title: Aurora Galactica Project Guide
strapline: A readable, current map of the project: what we are building, how the code and tooling fit together, and what still matters before the four-stage 1.0 release.
currentGoal: Ship a polished four-stage 1.0 slice: Stage 1, Stage 2, Stage 3 challenge, and a fair Stage 4 finish.

sections.title: Project Snapshot
sections.summary: The high-level state of the game and release effort right now.

cards.title: Current Product Target
cards.body: The current target is a consciously scoped 1.0: four playable stages, a readable challenge stage, one reliable capture/rescue loop, clean high-score/results flow, and public hosting that is trustworthy enough to share.

cards.title: Where The Game Is Strong
cards.body: Stage 1 and Stage 2 are in good shape, challenge-stage structure is manual-backed and measurably improved, rescue-to-dual flow is now testable end-to-end, and the project has release identity, synced public metadata, and a live release dashboard.

cards.title: Main Remaining Gameplay Blocker
cards.body: Stage 4 fairness is still the hardest knot before 1.0. The problem is narrower than before, but it still needs polish around escort pressure, collision fairness, and how the stage finishes under real play.

cards.title: How Decisions Are Made
cards.body: Reference material, harness evidence, and real play all matter. The current rule is to prefer original/manual-backed evidence when a fidelity question exists, then use harness runs and player captures to tune what the game feels like in practice.

sections.title: Release Scope And Status
sections.summary: What 1.0 means and how far we are from it.
bullets: 1.0 is the four-stage slice, not a full recreation of all Galaga content.
Stage 1 opening and descent feel are in acceptable shape for this release line.
Stage 2 opening pressure was softened without changing formation placement and now feels healthier.
Stage 3 challenge stage has manual-backed structure, measurable dwell-time coverage, and is now playing acceptably, though it is still polishable.
Stage 4 remains the release-critical gameplay blocker.
Capture/rescue works and has deterministic harness coverage, but the natural rescue flow still deserves another confidence pass.
The special boss-with-two-escorts bonus attack now appears naturally in real play, but its spacing and presentation still need polish before 1.0 is called done.

sections.title: Launch Blocking View
sections.summary: Compact release-triage view for the scoped four-stage 1.0 launch. Owner/status are intentionally short so the whole list stays visible.

rows: Hosted Stage 3->4 can still stall intermittently
Keep logging, capture next bad run
Challenge enemies firing breaks bonus rules
Challenge stage still not close enough to original
Capture/rescue still reads unclearly in play
Three-kill bonus group too spread out
Special squadrons still too loose visually
Prod and non-prod share score/data path
Choose env split and route writes
Capture rules improved, not fully original
Stage 1 feels fine manually, mixed harness
Self-play can still fail early in Stage 1
Score panel still wants stronger cues
Frame exists, art fidelity can wait
Mute button is useful but non-blocking

sections.title: Architecture Map
sections.summary: The main files and what they own.

cards.title: Game Shell
cards.body: `src/index.template.html` and `src/styles.css` define the page shell, settings UI, overlays, layout, and in-game presentation. The served `index.html` is generated from these sources during build.

cards.title: Boot / State / Metadata
cards.body: `src/js/00-boot.js` owns constants, build metadata, score/high-score state, audio, input handling, logging, and the UI state that surrounds the game loop.

cards.title: Gameplay Systems
cards.body: `src/js/10-gameplay.js` owns stage spawning, enemy movement, scoring, dive logic, capture/rescue, carried-fighter interactions, ship loss, and the main simulation loop.

cards.title: Rendering
cards.body: `src/js/20-render.js` owns sprite drawing, overlays, stage banners, HUD rendering, and the visual language that explains what is happening in play.

cards.title: Harness Hooks
cards.body: `src/js/90-harness.js` exposes deterministic setup helpers used by local Chrome harness scenarios so we can reproduce challenge, rescue, boss, and Stage 4 cases without depending only on random live play.

cards.title: Build / Public Sync
cards.body: `tools/build/build-index.js` assembles the playable game and generated pages. `tools/build/sync-public-pages.js` exports project status to the separate public-site repo using the agreed public status interface.

sections.title: Harness Coverage
sections.summary: What is currently measured automatically and why it matters.
bullets: Opening and pacing scenarios: `stage1-opening`, `stage2-opening`, `stage1-descent`.
Challenge fidelity scenario: `stage3-challenge`, including hit rate and upper-band dwell time.
Stage 4 guardrails: `stage4-five-ships` and `stage4-survival`.
Capture/rescue coverage: `rescue-dual`, `capture-rescue-dual`, `second-capture-current`, `repeat-capture-stage`, `carried-fighter-standby`, and `carried-fighter-attacking`.
Boss clarity coverage: `boss-first-hit`.
Special arcade-moment coverage: `stage4-squadron-bonus` and `stage12-variety`.
Run analysis now includes challenge, rescue, carried-fighter, later-stage, audio, and measured squadron-spacing metrics so regressions are easier to spot.

sections.title: Reference Baseline
sections.summary: The evidence sources the project treats as authoritative or useful.

cards.title: Primary Rule Sources
cards.body: The 1981 Namco manual and curated original gameplay footage are the first stop for rule questions, scoring behavior, challenge structure, capture/rescue behavior, and visible arcade moments.

cards.title: Secondary Sources
cards.body: Walkthroughs and written references are used carefully for later-stage breadth, progression feel, and confirming that the game experience expands beyond the first few stages, but they do not override primary sources on rules.

cards.title: Durable Analyses
cards.body: The project already keeps focused notes for the first challenge stage, Stage 4 fairness, manual observations, and capture/debug cases so we stop rediscovering the same lessons from scratch.

sections.title: Working Workflow
sections.summary: How changes are expected to move from idea to confidence.
bullets: Edit source files in `src/` and rebuild with `npm run build`.
Use harness scenarios or imported real-play sessions to validate behavior before treating a tuning pass as complete.
Push `main` only when the generated artifacts and release-facing pages are current and the repo is clean.
Use the release dashboard for milestone-level status, release notes for player-visible changes, and the public status export for the separate public project page.
When a behavior question is unclear, preserve the lesson in a durable note under `reference-artifacts/` or `reference-analysis/` instead of relying on chat history.

sections.title: Update Cadence
sections.summary: What updates automatically and what should be intentionally maintained.
bullets: Build identity updates on every build and appears in the game, settings, public pages, and generated status surfaces.
This project guide is regenerated on every build from maintained source content so the hosted page stays aligned with the current repository state.
The release dashboard page is regenerated on every build, but its milestone data only changes when `release-dashboard.json` is updated after a meaningful step.
Release notes should change only when there is a player-visible or milestone-relevant update worth calling out.
Public project status should update on pushes to `main` through the sync workflow, using the public status interface rather than direct homepage edits.

sections.title: Likely Next Polish Steps
sections.summary: The most likely next moves based on current project state.
bullets: Keep polishing Stage 4 fairness, especially escort pressure and how the stage finishes under real play.
Add the full natural capture-cycle harness scenario so rescue versus carried-fighter destruction is covered in the same sequence that players experience.
Do one more live-play polish pass on the special boss-and-two-escorts bonus attack now that spacing is tighter.
Keep high-score/results/initials and release/public surfaces clean so the four-stage 1.0 slice feels finished when gameplay is ready.
